His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11432358]] Wikidata name: Ōtomo no Kanamura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]] | regent = [[:en:Emperor Ninken|Emperor Ninken]] (until 498)
[[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] (499–507)
[[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
[[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] (536–539)
[[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}}

Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}}

[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11432358]] Wikidata name: Ōtomo no Kanamura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]] | regent = [[:en:Emperor Ninken|Emperor Ninken]] (until 498)
[[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] (499–507)
[[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
[[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] (536–539)
[[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11432358]] Wikidata name: Ōtomo no Kanamura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]] | regent = [[:en:Emperor Ninken|Emperor Ninken]] (until 498)
[[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] (499–507)
[[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
[[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] (536–539)
[[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of [[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]] (平群馬鳥) and in raising [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Emperor Buretsu]] to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]], instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son [[:en:Ōtomo no Satehiko|Ōtomo no Satehiko]] led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], under advice from the minister [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]], decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of {{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}} (平群馬鳥) and in raising {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}, instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}, under advice from the minister {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}}, decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}, instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}, under advice from the minister {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}}, decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 大伴 金村 | 画像 = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | 画像サイズ = 200px | 画像説明 = 大伴金村『[[前賢故実]]』より | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仁賢天皇]]→[[武烈天皇]]→[[継体天皇]]→[[安閑天皇]]→[[宣化天皇]]→[[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[大伴氏]] | 父母 = 父：[[大伴談]]または[[大伴室屋]] | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[大伴磐|磐]]、[[大伴狭手彦|狭手彦]]、[[大伴糠手子|糠手子]]、[[大伴阿被比古]]、
[[大伴宇遅古|宇遅古]]、[[大伴咋|咋]] | 特記事項 = }} '''大伴 金村'''（おおとも の かなむら）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]（[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]）。[[大伴室屋]]の孫で、[[大伴談]]の子とされるが、大伴室屋の子とする系図もある。 == 経歴 == 仁賢天皇11年（[[498年]]）[[仁賢天皇]]の[[崩御]]後に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]・[[平群真鳥]]、[[平群鮪|鮪]]父子を征討し、[[武烈天皇]]を即位させて自らは[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]の地位についた。武烈天皇8年（[[506年]]）武烈天皇の崩御により[[皇室の系図一覧|皇統]]は途絶えたが、[[応神天皇]]の玄孫とされる[[彦主人王]]の子を[[越前国]]から迎え[[継体天皇]]とし、以後[[安閑天皇|安閑]]・[[宣化天皇|宣化]]・[[欽明天皇|欽明]]の各[[天皇]]に仕えた。 『[[日本書紀]]』によると継体天皇6年（[[512年]]）に[[高句麗]]によって国土の北半分を奪われた[[百済]]からの[[任那]]4県割譲要請を受けて、金村はこれを承認する代わりに[[五経博士]]を渡来させた。継体天皇21年（[[527年]]）に発生した[[磐井の乱]]では[[物部麁鹿火]]を[[将軍]]に任命して鎮圧させた。 ただし、『[[古事記]]』の竺紫君石井退治（磐井の乱）の記事には、物部荒甲（麁鹿火）大連と大伴金村連が討伐にあたったと記されており、[[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』で単なる脱字の可能性と実はこの時にはまだ大連に就いていなかった可能性を指摘しており、近年においても笹川尚紀は『日本書紀』の編纂において、[[壬申の乱]]の功労者として当時の朝廷において一定の影響力を有していた大伴氏の伝承に基づく金村の顕彰記事（平郡真鳥討伐や継体天皇擁立など）が採用されていたと考え、金村は少なくとも継体天皇の即位の頃はまだ大連ではなかった可能性も指摘している笹川尚紀「『日本書紀』の編纂と大伴氏の伝承」（初出:『日本史研究』第600号（2012年）/笹川『日本書紀成立史攷』（塙書房、2016年）ISBN 978-4-8273-1281-2）。 しかし、欽明天皇の代に入ると欽明天皇と血縁関係を結んだ[[蘇我稲目]]が台頭、金村の権勢は衰え始める。さらに欽明天皇元年（[[540年]]）には[[新羅]]が任那地方を併合するという事件があり、[[物部尾輿]]などから外交政策の失敗（先の任那4県の割譲時に百済側から賄賂を受け取ったことなど）を糾弾され失脚して隠居する。これ以後、[[大伴氏]]は衰退していった。 晩年は大伴氏の館のあった[[摂津国]][[住吉郡]]（現在の[[大阪市]][[住吉区]]）に住み、そこで死去。住吉区にある[[帝塚山古墳]]は、大伴金村あるいはその子の墓とされている。ただし、古墳の築造年代は4世紀末から5世紀初頭であり年代が合わない。 [[奈良県]][[葛城市]]には、金村を祀る[[金村神社]]がある。 == 海外との関わり　== 金村が住吉に邸宅を構えていたのは、住吉が外交に適した地であったからであると考えられる「[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/ronsyuu7/5inoue.pdf]」。 == 系譜 == *父：[[大伴談]]「伴氏系図」（『[[続群書類従]]』巻第182所収）または[[大伴室屋]] 『古屋家家譜』（『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』資料編及び『各家系譜』所収） *母：不詳 *生母不明の子女 **男子：[[大伴磐]] **三男：[[大伴狭手彦]] **男子：[[大伴糠手子]] **男子：大伴阿彼布古『日本三代実録』貞観3年8月19日条 **男子：[[大伴宇遅古]] **男子：[[大伴咋]]『古屋家家譜』では阿彼布古の子とする。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == *[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 ==関連項目== *[[任那日本府]] *[[巨勢稲持]] *[[青海夫人勾子]] == 外部リンク == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20030216103310/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観　大伴氏／伴氏【1】] {{DEFAULTSORT:おおとも の かなむら}} [[Category:大伴氏|かなむら]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:大和国の人物]] [[Category:摂津国の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] [[Category:6世紀日本の政治家]]

revision.text:
[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}} | regent = {{ill|Emperor Ninken|en|Emperor Ninken|WD=Q357266|lt=Emperor Ninken}} (until 498)
{{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} (499–507)
{{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
{{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} (536–539)
{{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}} (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of {{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}} (平群馬鳥) and in raising {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}, instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}, under advice from the minister {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}}, decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{translated page|ja|大伴金村|version=106024247|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 大伴 金村 | 画像 = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | 画像サイズ = 200px | 画像説明 = 大伴金村『[[前賢故実]]』より | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仁賢天皇]]→[[武烈天皇]]→[[継体天皇]]→[[安閑天皇]]→[[宣化天皇]]→[[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[大伴氏]] | 父母 = 父：[[大伴談]]または[[大伴室屋]] | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[大伴磐|磐]]、[[大伴狭手彦|狭手彦]]、[[大伴糠手子|糠手子]]、[[大伴阿被比古]]、
[[大伴宇遅古|宇遅古]]、[[大伴咋|咋]] | 特記事項 = }} '''大伴 金村'''（おおとも の かなむら）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]（[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]）。[[大伴室屋]]の孫で、[[大伴談]]の子とされるが、大伴室屋の子とする系図もある。 == 経歴 == 仁賢天皇11年（[[498年]]）[[仁賢天皇]]の[[崩御]]後に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]・[[平群真鳥]]、[[平群鮪|鮪]]父子を征討し、[[武烈天皇]]を即位させて自らは[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]の地位についた。武烈天皇8年（[[506年]]）武烈天皇の崩御により[[皇室の系図一覧|皇統]]は途絶えたが、[[応神天皇]]の玄孫とされる[[彦主人王]]の子を[[越前国]]から迎え[[継体天皇]]とし、以後[[安閑天皇|安閑]]・[[宣化天皇|宣化]]・[[欽明天皇|欽明]]の各[[天皇]]に仕えた。 『[[日本書紀]]』によると継体天皇6年（[[512年]]）に[[高句麗]]によって国土の北半分を奪われた[[百済]]からの[[任那]]4県割譲要請を受けて、金村はこれを承認する代わりに[[五経博士]]を渡来させた。継体天皇21年（[[527年]]）に発生した[[磐井の乱]]では[[物部麁鹿火]]を[[将軍]]に任命して鎮圧させた。 ただし、『[[古事記]]』の竺紫君石井退治（磐井の乱）の記事には、物部荒甲（麁鹿火）大連と大伴金村連が討伐にあたったと記されており、[[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』で単なる脱字の可能性と実はこの時にはまだ大連に就いていなかった可能性を指摘しており、近年においても笹川尚紀は『日本書紀』の編纂において、[[壬申の乱]]の功労者として当時の朝廷において一定の影響力を有していた大伴氏の伝承に基づく金村の顕彰記事（平郡真鳥討伐や継体天皇擁立など）が採用されていたと考え、金村は少なくとも継体天皇の即位の頃はまだ大連ではなかった可能性も指摘している笹川尚紀「『日本書紀』の編纂と大伴氏の伝承」（初出:『日本史研究』第600号（2012年）/笹川『日本書紀成立史攷』（塙書房、2016年）ISBN 978-4-8273-1281-2）。 しかし、欽明天皇の代に入ると欽明天皇と血縁関係を結んだ[[蘇我稲目]]が台頭、金村の権勢は衰え始める。さらに欽明天皇元年（[[540年]]）には[[新羅]]が任那地方を併合するという事件があり、[[物部尾輿]]などから外交政策の失敗（先の任那4県の割譲時に百済側から賄賂を受け取ったことなど）を糾弾され失脚して隠居する。これ以後、[[大伴氏]]は衰退していった。 晩年は大伴氏の館のあった[[摂津国]][[住吉郡]]（現在の[[大阪市]][[住吉区]]）に住み、そこで死去。住吉区にある[[帝塚山古墳]]は、大伴金村あるいはその子の墓とされている。ただし、古墳の築造年代は4世紀末から5世紀初頭であり年代が合わない。 [[奈良県]][[葛城市]]には、金村を祀る[[金村神社]]がある。 == 海外との関わり　== 金村が住吉に邸宅を構えていたのは、住吉が外交に適した地であったからであると考えられる「[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/ronsyuu7/5inoue.pdf]」。 == 系譜 == *父：[[大伴談]]「伴氏系図」（『[[続群書類従]]』巻第182所収）または[[大伴室屋]] 『古屋家家譜』（『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』資料編及び『各家系譜』所収） *母：不詳 *生母不明の子女 **男子：[[大伴磐]] **三男：[[大伴狭手彦]] **男子：[[大伴糠手子]] **男子：大伴阿彼布古『日本三代実録』貞観3年8月19日条 **男子：[[大伴宇遅古]] **男子：[[大伴咋]]『古屋家家譜』では阿彼布古の子とする。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == *[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 ==関連項目== *[[任那日本府]] *[[巨勢稲持]] *[[青海夫人勾子]] == 外部リンク == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20030216103310/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観　大伴氏／伴氏【1】] {{DEFAULTSORT:おおとも の かなむら}} [[Category:大伴氏|かなむら]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:大和国の人物]] [[Category:摂津国の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] [[Category:6世紀日本の政治家]] ==End Japanese==

[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147|1=Otomo no Uchiko|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}} | regent = {{ill|Emperor Ninken|en|Emperor Ninken|WD=Q357266|lt=Emperor Ninken}} (until 498)
{{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} (499–507)
{{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
{{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} (536–539)
{{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}} (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of {{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}} (平群馬鳥) and in raising {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}, instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}, under advice from the minister {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}}, decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{translated page|ja|大伴金村|version=106024247|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 大伴 金村 | 画像 = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | 画像サイズ = 200px | 画像説明 = 大伴金村『[[前賢故実]]』より | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仁賢天皇]]→[[武烈天皇]]→[[継体天皇]]→[[安閑天皇]]→[[宣化天皇]]→[[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[大伴氏]] | 父母 = 父：[[大伴談]]または[[大伴室屋]] | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[大伴磐|磐]]、[[大伴狭手彦|狭手彦]]、[[大伴糠手子|糠手子]]、[[大伴阿被比古]]、

[[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]][[ja:大伴金村]][[th:โอโตโมะ โนะ คานามูระ]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Otomono Kanamura.jpg|birth_date=0550|death_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi district|WD=Q10885872}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|warrior|WD=Q1250916}}|title={{ill|Ōmuraji|WD=Q4328807}}|children={{ill|Q118727147|WD=Q118727147|1=Otomo no Uchiko|WD=Q118727147}}; {{ill|Q97819087|WD=Q97819087|1=Otomo no Abi-hiko|WD=Q97819087}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Iwa|WD=Q30932418}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Kui|WD=Q30932358}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Nukateko|WD=Q30932398}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483}}|father={{ill|Muroya Ootomo|WD=Q11432336}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Katari|WD=Q11432354}}|family={{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938}}|native_name=大伴金村}} {{Short description|Japanese warrior-statesman of late Kofun period}} {{expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=大伴金村|date=October 2014}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Ōtomo no Kanamura
大伴 金村 | image = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Kanamura, by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}} | regent = {{ill|Emperor Ninken|en|Emperor Ninken|WD=Q357266|lt=Emperor Ninken}} (until 498)
{{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} (499–507)
{{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (507–531)
[[Emperor Ankan]] (531–536)
{{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} (536–539)
{{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}} (after 539) | reg-type = Lord(s) | issue = 6, including {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} | era dates = [[Kofun period]] | house = [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]] | house-type = Clan }} '''Ōtomo no Kanamura''' (大伴金村) was a Japanese warrior and statesman during the late [[Kofun period]].{{cite book|author1=Louis Frédéric|title=Japan Encyclopedia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA764|year=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|pages=764}} Most of what is known of his life comes from the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. His clan, the [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo]], had been highly influential at court since the time of his grandfather [[Ōtomo no Muroya]].{{cite book|author1=Paula Doe|author2=Yakamochi Ōtomo|title=A Warbler's Song in the Dusk: The Life and Work of Ōtomo Yakamochi (718-785)|url=https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep|url-access=registration|date=January 1982|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-04346-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/warblerssongindu0000doep/page/6 6]–7}}{{cite book|title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan: Niju-Saka|url=https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda|url-access=registration|year=1983|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=978-0-87011-626-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/kodanshaencyclop0000koda/page/130 130]–131}} According to these sources, Kanamura was instrumental in putting down the uprising of {{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}} (平群馬鳥) and in raising {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}} to the throne.{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=Japan: A Short Cultural History|url=https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich|url-access=registration|year=1978|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0954-5|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanshortcultur00sansrich/page/75 75]}} Buretsu, in gratitude, raised Kanamura to the position of [[Muraji|Ōmuraji]] (a high-Ranking ministerial position).{{cite book|author=Jien|title=The Future and the Past: A Translation and Study of the Gukanshō, an Interpretative History of Japan Written in 1219|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA260|date=1 January 1979|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0-520-03460-0|pages=260}} He also oversaw the succession of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}, instead of the claimant Prince Yamatohiko, and selected Keitai's empress himself.{{cite book|author=Yoko Williams|title=Tsumi - Offence and Retribution in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QVtUAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|date=11 October 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-87422-2|pages=228}}{{cite book|author=Sir George Bailey Sansom|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|year=1958|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-0523-3|pages=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/44 44]}} Kanamura embraced an aggressive policy towards the kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} (part of modern-day Korea), and advocated sending forces there; his own son {{ill|Ōtomo no Satehiko|en|Ōtomo no Satehiko|WD=Q15043483|lt=Ōtomo no Satehiko}} led two expeditions against the Korean kingdoms.{{cite book|author1=Robert Karl Reischauer|author2=Jean Reischauer|author3=Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs|title=Early Japanese history, c. 40 B.C.-A.D. 1167|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jy0QAQAAMAAJ|year=1967|page=133|publisher=P. Smith}} This policy eventually led to his downfall, when in 540 the {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}, under advice from the minister {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}}, decided to refrain from direct military action against Silla. The Emperor also removed Kanamura from his position as Ōmuraji as a result.{{cite book|author=John Whitney Hall|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&pg=PA155|date=30 July 1993|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=155}} ==References== {{Reflist}} [[en:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[es:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[fr:Ōtomo no Kanamura]] [[ja:大伴金村]] {{wikidata link|Q11432358}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{translated page|ja|大伴金村|version=106024247|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 大伴 金村 | 画像 = Otomono Kanamura.jpg | 画像サイズ = 200px | 画像説明 = 大伴金村『[[前賢故実]]』より | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仁賢天皇]]→[[武烈天皇]]→[[継体天皇]]→[[安閑天皇]]→[[宣化天皇]]→[[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[大伴氏]] | 父母 = 父：[[大伴談]]または[[大伴室屋]] | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[大伴磐|磐]]、[[大伴狭手彦|狭手彦]]、[[大伴糠手子|糠手子]]、[[大伴阿被比古]]、
[[大伴宇遅古|宇遅古]]、[[大伴咋|咋]] | 特記事項 = }} '''大伴 金村'''（おおとも の かなむら）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]（[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]）。[[大伴室屋]]の孫で、[[大伴談]]の子とされるが、大伴室屋の子とする系図もある。 == 経歴 == 仁賢天皇11年（[[498年]]）[[仁賢天皇]]の[[崩御]]後に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]・[[平群真鳥]]、[[平群鮪|鮪]]父子を征討し、[[武烈天皇]]を即位させて自らは[[大連 (古代日本)|大連]]の地位についた。武烈天皇8年（[[506年]]）武烈天皇の崩御により[[皇室の系図一覧|皇統]]は途絶えたが、[[応神天皇]]の玄孫とされる[[彦主人王]]の子を[[越前国]]から迎え[[継体天皇]]とし、以後[[安閑天皇|安閑]]・[[宣化天皇|宣化]]・[[欽明天皇|欽明]]の各[[天皇]]に仕えた。 『[[日本書紀]]』によると継体天皇6年（[[512年]]）に[[高句麗]]によって国土の北半分を奪われた[[百済]]からの[[任那]]4県割譲要請を受けて、金村はこれを承認する代わりに[[五経博士]]を渡来させた。継体天皇21年（[[527年]]）に発生した[[磐井の乱]]では[[物部麁鹿火]]を[[将軍]]に任命して鎮圧させた。 ただし、『[[古事記]]』の竺紫君石井退治（磐井の乱）の記事には、物部荒甲（麁鹿火）大連と大伴金村連が討伐にあたったと記されており、[[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』で単なる脱字の可能性と実はこの時にはまだ大連に就いていなかった可能性を指摘しており、近年においても笹川尚紀は『日本書紀』の編纂において、[[壬申の乱]]の功労者として当時の朝廷において一定の影響力を有していた大伴氏の伝承に基づく金村の顕彰記事（平郡真鳥討伐や継体天皇擁立など）が採用されていたと考え、金村は少なくとも継体天皇の即位の頃はまだ大連ではなかった可能性も指摘している笹川尚紀「『日本書紀』の編纂と大伴氏の伝承」（初出:『日本史研究』第600号（2012年）/笹川『日本書紀成立史攷』（塙書房、2016年）ISBN 978-4-8273-1281-2）。 しかし、欽明天皇の代に入ると欽明天皇と血縁関係を結んだ[[蘇我稲目]]が台頭、金村の権勢は衰え始める。さらに欽明天皇元年（[[540年]]）には[[新羅]]が任那地方を併合するという事件があり、[[物部尾輿]]などから外交政策の失敗（先の任那4県の割譲時に百済側から賄賂を受け取ったことなど）を糾弾され失脚して隠居する。これ以後、[[大伴氏]]は衰退していった。 晩年は大伴氏の館のあった[[摂津国]][[住吉郡]]（現在の[[大阪市]][[住吉区]]）に住み、そこで死去。住吉区にある[[帝塚山古墳]]は、大伴金村あるいはその子の墓とされている。ただし、古墳の築造年代は4世紀末から5世紀初頭であり年代が合わない。 [[奈良県]][[葛城市]]には、金村を祀る[[金村神社]]がある。 == 海外との関わり　== 金村が住吉に邸宅を構えていたのは、住吉が外交に適した地であったからであると考えられる「[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/ronsyuu7/5inoue.pdf]」。 == 系譜 == *父：[[大伴談]]「伴氏系図」（『[[続群書類従]]』巻第182所収）または[[大伴室屋]] 『古屋家家譜』（『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』資料編及び『各家系譜』所収） *母：不詳 *生母不明の子女 **男子：[[大伴磐]] **三男：[[大伴狭手彦]] **男子：[[大伴糠手子]] **男子：大伴阿彼布古『日本三代実録』貞観3年8月19日条 **男子：[[大伴宇遅古]] **男子：[[大伴咋]]『古屋家家譜』では阿彼布古の子とする。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == *[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 ==関連項目== *[[任那日本府]] *[[巨勢稲持]] *[[青海夫人勾子]] == 外部リンク == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20030216103310/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観　大伴氏／伴氏【1】] {{DEFAULTSORT:おおとも の かなむら}} [[Category:大伴氏|かなむら]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:大和国の人物]] [[Category:摂津国の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] [[Category:6世紀日本の政治家]] ==End Japanese==
